from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister
import numpy as np

def create_circuit(secret_int, N=3):
    qr = QuantumRegister(N, 'x')
    anc = QuantumRegister(1, 'anc')
    cr = ClassicalRegister(N, 'c')
    qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, anc, cr)

    # Prepare superposition
    qc.h(qr)

    # Phase kickback/Oracle
    # Condition: f(x) = 1 if x != secret_int, 0 if x == secret_int
    # We implement the oracle for f(x) = 0 if x == secret_int
    # 1. Flip bits to match secret_int
    bin_secret = format(secret_int, f'0{N}b')[::-1]
    for i, bit in enumerate(bin_secret):
        if bit == '0':
            qc.x(qr[i])

    # 2. Multi-controlled X to ancilla
    qc.x(anc)
    qc.h(anc)
    qc.mcx(qr, anc)
    qc.h(anc)
    qc.x(anc)

    # 3. Uncompute
    for i, bit in enumerate(bin_secret):
        if bit == '0':
            qc.x(qr[i])

    # Post-gates: Grover diffusion
    qc.h(qr)
    qc.x(qr)
    qc.h(qr[N-1])
    qc.mcx(qr[:-1], qr[N-1])
    qc.h(qr[N-1])
    qc.x(qr)
    qc.h(qr)

    qc.measure(qr, cr)
    return qc

As N grows, the quantum approach yields a quadratic speedup over classical exhaustive search.

In hash function security, this relates to finding pre-images of functions designed to be one-way under specific modular constraints.
